title: Docker Fundamentals Quiz
description: Test your Docker knowledge with questions on containers, images, Dockerfile instructions, volumes, networks, Compose, and basic orchestration concepts.

questions.title: Dockerfile Best Practices
questions.description: Choose the most efficient Dockerfile for a Node.js application.
questions.situation: You need to create a Dockerfile for a Node.js web application that has dependencies in package.json.
questions.codeExample:
// Your app structure:
├── package.json
├── package-lock.json
├── src/
│   └── app.js
└── public/
    └── index.html
options:
FROM node:16-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

FROM node:16
COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN npm install
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

FROM node:16
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN npm install
CMD ["node", "src/app.js"]

FROM ubuntu
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y nodejs npm
COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN npm install
CMD ["npm", "start"]
questions.explanation: Option B uses Alpine for smaller image size, leverages Docker layer caching by copying package files first, uses npm ci for production builds, and follows proper WORKDIR practices.
questions.hint: Consider image size, layer caching, and production best practices. Which approach optimizes for both build speed and security?

questions.title: Docker Networking
questions.description: How should two containers communicate in a multi-container application?
questions.situation: You have a web application container that needs to connect to a PostgreSQL database container.
options: Use --link flag when running containers
Use localhost and map ports to host
Create a custom bridge network and connect both containers to it
Connect containers using IP addresses
questions.explanation: Custom bridge networks provide better isolation, automatic DNS resolution between containers, and are the recommended approach for multi-container applications.
questions.hint: Think about modern Docker networking practices. What's the current recommended way to connect containers?

questions.title: Data Persistence
questions.description: What's the best way to persist database data in a containerized PostgreSQL?
questions.situation: You're running PostgreSQL in a container and need to ensure data persists across container restarts and updates.
options: Use bind mounts to map host directory
Copy data files during container build
Use --volumes-from another container
Create a named volume and mount it to /var/lib/postgresql/data
questions.explanation: Named volumes are managed by Docker, provide better performance, work across different host systems, and are the recommended way to persist database data.
questions.hint: Consider portability, performance, and Docker's management capabilities. What's the most robust solution?

questions.title: Optimizing Image Size
questions.description: You need to build a Go application with minimal final image size.
questions.situation: Your Go application needs to be compiled, but you don't want build tools in the final production image.
questions.codeExample:
// You have:
- main.go (your application)
- go.mod and go.sum (dependencies)
- Need: minimal production image
options:
FROM golang:1.19
COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN go build -o myapp
CMD ["./myapp"]

FROM golang:1.19 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.* ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN go build -o myapp

FROM alpine:latest
RUN apk --no-cache add ca-certificates
COPY --from=builder /app/myapp /
CMD ["/myapp"]

FROM alpine:latest
RUN apk add --no-cache go
COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN go build -o myapp
CMD ["./myapp"]

FROM scratch
COPY main.go /
CMD ["go", "run", "main.go"]
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds allow you to use a full build environment in the first stage, then copy only the compiled binary to a minimal final image, significantly reducing size.
questions.hint: Think about separating the build environment from the runtime environment. How can you get the benefits of both?

questions.title: Container Health Monitoring
questions.description: How do you properly implement health checks for a web application container?
questions.situation: Your web application runs on port 3000 and has a /health endpoint that returns HTTP 200 when healthy.
options: HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
RUN curl -f http://localhost:3000/health
HEALTHCHECK CMD ps aux | grep node
No health check needed - Docker handles this automatically
questions.explanation: The HEALTHCHECK instruction with proper interval, timeout, and retry settings using the application's health endpoint is the correct approach for monitoring container health.
questions.hint: Consider what makes a container truly healthy - is it just running, or actually serving requests correctly?

questions.title: Container Security
questions.description: What's the most important security practice when building production Docker images?
questions.situation: You're preparing a Docker image for production deployment and need to follow security best practices.
options: Always run as root user for maximum compatibility
Create and use a non-root user, avoid unnecessary packages, and use specific image tags
Use the latest tag for all base images to get updates
Install all possible dependencies to avoid runtime issues
questions.explanation: Running as non-root, minimizing attack surface by avoiding unnecessary packages, and using specific tags (not 'latest') are fundamental Docker security practices.
questions.hint: Think about the principle of least privilege and minimizing the attack surface. What practices reduce security risks?

questions.title: Images vs Containers
questions.description: Use the core Docker vocabulary correctly.
questions.situation: One teammate says 'just run the image' and another says 'restart the container'. You want to use the terms precisely.
options: They are two names for the same thing
A container is a read-only template and an image is a running instance of it
An image is a read-only template, and a container is a runnable instance created from that image
An image can only ever produce a single container
questions.explanation: An image is an immutable, layered template. You create one or many containers from a single image; each container is an isolated instance with its own writable layer. Think class versus instance.
questions.hint: Think class versus instance in programming.

questions.title: Running Your First Container
questions.description: Start a container in the background with a published port.
questions.situation: You want to run the official nginx image in the background and reach it on host port 8080.
options: docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx
docker start -d -p 8080:80 nginx
docker run -p 80:8080 nginx
docker exec -d -p 8080:80 nginx
questions.explanation: docker run creates and starts a new container. -d runs it detached (in the background), and -p 8080:80 publishes host port 8080 to container port 80. docker start only restarts an existing container; docker exec runs a command in a running one.

questions.title: Listing Containers
questions.description: Find a container that exited immediately.
questions.situation: You started a container but `docker ps` shows nothing. It seems to have exited right away.
options: docker images, which lists your containers
docker logs, which lists all containers
Nothing, an exited container is gone for good
docker ps -a, which includes stopped and exited containers
questions.explanation: docker ps shows only running containers. Add -a (all) to include stopped and exited ones, then docker logs <id> tells you why it exited. docker images lists images, not containers.
questions.hint: ps shows running only; one flag shows everything.

questions.title: Building an Image
questions.description: Build and tag an image from a Dockerfile.
questions.situation: You have a Dockerfile in the current directory and want an image named myapp with tag v1.
options: docker run -t myapp:v1 .
docker build -t myapp:v1 .
docker build myapp:v1
docker create myapp:v1 .
questions.explanation: docker build builds an image from a Dockerfile. -t sets the name:tag and the trailing . is the build context (the current directory). Leaving off the context path is one of the most common first-time errors.
questions.hint: -t name:tag, and do not forget the build context path at the end.

questions.title: Dockerfile Instructions
questions.description: Know what the core Dockerfile instructions do.
questions.situation: You are reading a Dockerfile for the first time and want to understand the foundational instruction.
options: FROM, which sets the base image the build starts from
RUN, which starts the container
CMD, which copies your application files in
EXPOSE, which downloads the base image
questions.explanation: A Dockerfile starts FROM a base image (aside from an optional leading ARG). RUN executes build-time commands, COPY brings files into the image, and CMD sets the default command the container runs at startup.
questions.hint: Every image is built on top of another image.

questions.title: Docker Compose Basics
questions.description: Understand what `docker compose up` does for a multi-service app.
questions.situation: Your compose.yaml defines a web service and a db service.
questions.codeExample:
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
  db:
    image: postgres:16
options: It builds a single image containing both web and db
It starts only the first service listed
It creates and starts both services on a shared network so they reach each other by service name
It runs both services but keeps them on isolated networks by default
questions.explanation: docker compose up brings every defined service up together and puts them on a shared default network, so web can reach the database at the hostname 'db'. It is the standard way to run a multi-container app locally.
questions.hint: One command, all services, one network, reachable by service name.

questions.title: Publishing Ports
questions.description: Get the host-to-container port mapping the right way round.
questions.situation: Your app listens on port 3000 inside the container. You run `docker run -p 3000:8080 myapp` and cannot reach it at localhost:3000.
options: Containers cannot publish ports below 8080
The mapping is backwards: -p is host:container, so you sent host 3000 to container 8080 where nothing listens. Use -p 3000:3000
You must add -d or published ports are ignored
localhost is never reachable from a container
questions.explanation: The -p flag uses host:container order. -p 3000:8080 forwards host port 3000 to container port 8080, but the app listens on 3000 inside, so nothing answers. Use -p 3000:3000 (or -p 8080:3000 to reach it on host port 8080).
questions.hint: host:container. Which side is the app actually listening on?

questions.title: Getting a Shell in a Container
questions.description: Open an interactive shell inside a running container.
questions.situation: A container is running and you want a shell inside it to look around and debug.
options: docker run -it <container> sh, to reuse the running container
docker attach <container> sh
docker shell <container>
docker exec -it <container> sh
questions.explanation: docker exec runs a new command in an already-running container, and -it gives you an interactive terminal. docker run would start a brand new container instead, and docker attach connects to the main process rather than opening a new shell.
questions.hint: exec runs in an existing container; run makes a new one.
